slides.narration: Yo, what's up everybody! Professor Davis here from Yale SOM. Today, bro, we are diving deep into AI screenplays. You know, like, wouldn't it be cool if we could get AWESOM-O here to write a whole movie for us? Not just a scene, but a coherent, truly awesome screenplay? We're gonna build out an agentic flow to do exactly that, right?
slides.slide_description: The slide features the title "AI Screenplays" in large, bold dark blue text centered at the top. Below the title is a large illustrative cartoon image, characteristic of South Park animation. The image depicts a lavish office with a well-dressed man (Randy Marsh) standing behind a large wooden desk, gesturing towards a globe. On the desk are various awards including two Oscar statues, an Emmy award, and a red trophy shaped like Cartman. Other items on the desk include a black rotary phone, a brown notebook, and a stack of papers labeled "SCRIPT REVIEWS". In front of the desk, facing the viewer, is a yellow cardboard robot character named "AWESOM-O". Behind the desk, a large arched window overlooks a sunset landscape featuring the Hollywood sign. To the right of the standing man, a fish tank contains a grey alien figure, a safe is visible, and a framed family photo (likely the Marsh family) rests on a side table. A framed picture on the far right wall displays "AWESOME-O" with the word "LAME" in a red box underneath. The overall scene suggests a context of Hollywood, scriptwriting, and potential comedic or critical commentary on AI in entertainment.

slides.narration: Alright, so, we're here to talk about generating long documents with AI, right? And I mean *long* documents, like, not just a couple of sentences, but actual books, reports, full-on screenplays and stuff. That's the dream, right? Today, specifically, dude, we're gonna build out this super cool 'agentic flow' to generate screenplays for movies, step by step. But get this, bro: once we've got that down, we're gonna show you how this exact same flow architecture can be generalized to pretty much *any* other long text document. So, like and subscribe, 'cause it's gonna be awesome.
slides.slide_description: The slide features the title "Generating Long Documents" in large, bold dark blue text at the top, consistent with the presentation's theme of AI in screenplays. Below the title, there are four black bullet points. The first bullet states: "AI can be used to generate long, coherent text documents". The second bullet provides examples: "Books, reports, screenplays". The third bullet outlines the day's objective: "Today we will build an agentic flow to generate screenplays for movies". The final bullet point indicates the broader applicability of the method: "This flow architecture can be generalized to other long text documents". The slide serves as an introduction to the capabilities and scope of the AI document generation discussion, building on the general 'AI Screenplays' theme introduced in the first slide.

slides.narration: Okay, so, generating long documents sounds amazing, right? But here's the catch, bro, here's the big challenge: "one-shot" long-form generation is super difficult for an AI. Like, she's really good at writing small parts, right? Little paragraphs, sentences, even a whole page sometimes. But try to get her to write a whole novel or a full screenplay in one go? Nah, dude. She totally becomes "lost in the middle," you know? You get deep into the document and there's this thing called "context drift." She starts to hallucinate, forgets what she's supposed to be writing about, totally loses focus. It's a mess, right?
slides.slide_description: The slide features a prominent title at the top, centered and in bold dark blue text, reading "Challenge: "One-Shot" Long-Form". Below the title, two main bullet points detail challenges in AI document generation, continuing the theme from the previous slide about generating long documents. The first bullet states: "It is difficult for an AI to generate a long coherent document in one-shot". The second main bullet point reads: "Local vs global – the AI is good at writing small parts, but cannot maintain coherence across whole document". This second point is further elaborated with two sub-points, indicated by dashes: "– Becomes “lost in the middle”" and "– Context drift – deep into the document, the AI can hallucinate or lose focus of what to write". All text for the bullet points is in bold black font. The background of the slide is a plain light grey.

slides.narration: Okay, so, to combat that 'lost in the middle' problem we just talked about, right? Movie screenplays are actually perfect for this, dude. They have so many different elements the AI needs to capture, like, the overall story shape, the goals of each 'sequence' in the movie, and then all the specific scene action and dialogue, blah blah blah. But the cool part is, they *already have* a super clear, hierarchical structure built-in, bro. You start with the Premise, then the big Story arc, then Sequences, and finally, down to individual Scenes. It's like a roadmap, you know? This is what we're gonna leverage.
slides.slide_description: The slide features the title "Movie Screenplays" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top. This slide elaborates on the components of screenplays, continuing the theme of generating long documents and screenplays from previous slides. The content is presented in two main bullet points, each with several sub-points. The first main bullet point, in bold black text, states: "Screenplays have many different elements the AI needs to capture". It is followed by three sub-points, indicated by dashes: "– Story shape", "– Goals of “sequences” in the movie", and "– Scene action and dialogue". The second main bullet point, also in bold black text, reads: "Hierarchical structure of a screenplay". It is followed by four sub-points: "– Premise – basic elements of the movie", "– Story arc – high level plot structure", "– Sequences - more detailed plot", and "– Scenes – even more detailed plot". The background of the slide is plain light grey.

slides.narration: Alright, so, we talked about that hierarchical structure, right? This is how we visualize it, bro, our 'Screenplay Flow.' We start with the Premise, which is like, premise dot json, right? That feeds into our Arc, the arc dot json. From the Arc, dude, it branches out. It informs both the Sequences, sequence dot json, and then the individual Scenes, scenes dot json. See how it flows? Each step builds on the last, passing that crucial context along in these neat little JSON files. Pretty cool, huh?
slides.slide_description:
The slide features the title "Screenplay Flow" in large, bold dark blue text centered at the top, similar in style to previous slide titles. The main content is a horizontal flow diagram illustrating the hierarchical structure of a screenplay, visually representing the concepts introduced in the previous slide. The diagram consists of four distinct, colorful rectangular boxes, each with a small chest icon on its left side and text representing a stage in the screenplay process, followed by a corresponding '.json' filename.

The flow begins with a blue box labeled "Premise" with "premise.json" below it. An arrow points from "Premise" to a purple box labeled "Arc" with "arc.json" below it. From "Arc", two arrows branch out: one pointing horizontally to the right to an orange box labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json" below it, and another arrow pointing downwards to a green box labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json" below it. This visual layout suggests a progression from a high-level premise to a detailed scene structure, with 'Arc' being a central element connecting to both 'Sequences' and 'Scenes'. The '.json' file names suggest these components might be generated or stored as separate data files.

slides.narration: Okay, so, starting with our first step in that Screenplay Flow, dude, we've got our 'Premise Agent.' This is like, a chat agent, right? She's gonna discuss with you what your movie is *actually* about. We're talking title, the logline – which is kinda like your executive summary, you know? – characters, genre, the central conflict, all that good stuff. And if you're totally stuck, you can even ask her for plot ideas, which is super cool. It takes a few minutes, even if you have a general sense, 'cause you gotta specify all those major elements, right? And then, boom, she creates this file: `premise.json`.
slides.slide_description:
The slide is titled "Premise Agent" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top, continuing the presentation's theme of AI in screenplays. In the top right corner, a small image of the yellow cardboard robot character, AWESOM-O, is visible, an element introduced in the first slide. The content of the slide describes the function of the "Premise Agent" using four main bullet points in bold black text, elaborating on the 'Premise' stage introduced in the previous 'Screenplay Flow' diagram.

The first bullet point states: "A chat agent that discusses with you what your movie is about". This is followed by a sub-bullet, indicated by a dash, listing the elements discussed: "– Title, logline (like an executive summary), characters, genre, central conflict".

The second bullet point explains: "You can ask the agent to give ideas for the plot if you are stuck".

The third bullet point provides context on interaction time: "This chat can take a few minutes even if you have a general sense about your movie (takes time to specify all the major elements of the movie)".

The final bullet point details the output of the agent: "This agent then creates a file premise.json", directly linking back to the 'premise.json' file mentioned in the 'Screenplay Flow' of slide 5. The background is a plain light grey.

slides.narration: So, here it is, dude, the actual output from that Premise Agent: `premise.json`. See, we've got the project ID, the title: "Shadow of the Dark World." And check out that logline, bro: "A seemingly ordinary young man, Link, is thrust into a perilous quest..." blah blah blah. It's a full executive summary right there, generated by Awesome-O, right? It even nails the genre, like fantasy, action, adventure, and all the themes, 'hero's journey' and stuff. This is the foundation, you know? This is what gives us all that global context for everything else we're gonna build.
slides.slide_description:
The slide features the title "Premise.json" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top, consistent with the presentation's style. Below the title, a large dark rectangular block displays a JSON object, presenting an example of the 'premise.json' file discussed in the previous slide. The JSON content includes:
-   "project_id": "shadow_of_the_dark_world_20260402_025456"
-   "title": "Shadow of the Dark World"
-   "writers": ["TBD"]
-   "source_script": null
-   "format_notes": "Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat."
-   "logline": "A seemingly ordinary young man, Link, is thrust into a perilous quest after his uncle is gravely wounded rescuing Princess Zelda. He must gather legendary artifacts, wield the fabled Master Sword, and confront a malevolent wizard to prevent the dark lord Ganon's release and the conquest of their world."
-   "genre": ["fantasy", "action", "adventure"]
-   "themes": ["hero's journey", "destiny", "courage in the face of overwhelming evil", "good versus evil", "sacrifice"]

The logline provides a detailed narrative summary, and the format notes indicate the content was AI-generated. This slide serves as a concrete example of the output from the 'Premise Agent' described in slide 6, illustrating the structured data generated for a movie's core concept.

slides.narration: Okay, so check it out, still on `premise.json` for our 'Shadow of the Dark World' screenplay, right? This part, dude, really breaks down the 'central conflict.' Link's gotta stop Aghanim from sacrificing Zelda and all these other maidens to prevent Ganon from, like, totally unleashing the Dark World. And she defines the setting, bro, like 'High fantasy era,' and lists key places: Hyrule Castle, Kakariko Village. Plus, it gives us our protagonist, Link: his role as an 'unassuming hero destined to wield the Master Sword,' and what he *wants* – to save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and stop Ganon. So much rich context here, dude!
slides.slide_description:
The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top, consistent with the presentation's style. Similar to the previous slide, the main content is a large dark rectangular block displaying a JSON object, providing further details for the example 'premise.json' file. This JSON object continues to define core story elements, likely for the 'Shadow of the Dark World' concept.

Key elements within the JSON include:
-   "central_conflict": Describing Link's role as a hero, stopping Aganhim from sacrificing Princess Zelda and six other maidens, and preventing Ganon from unleashing the Dark World.
-   "setting": An object containing "primary_time" ("High fantasy era, contemporary-feel for characters") and "primary_places" (listing "Hyrule Castle", "Kakariko Village", "Eastern Palace", "Lost Woods", "Dark World").
-   "protagonists": An array containing an object for "Link", detailing his "name", "role" ("Unassuming hero, destined to wield the Master Sword"), and "want" ("To save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and ultimately prevent Aganhim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule").

This slide further elaborates on the structured data output from the 'Premise Agent', demonstrating a more complete example of a story premise in JSON format, building upon the introductory elements shown in slide 7.

slides.narration: And we're still on `premise.json` here, dude! So, after Link, it defines our 'key antagonist,' Aghanim, right? Evil sorcerer, Ganon's pawn, wants to seal the seven maidens, including Zelda, to open a portal and release Ganon. Super evil, you know? Then, our 'key ally,' Sahasrahla, the wise village elder, who wants to guide Link. And boom, the 'stakes': total conquest of the Light World by Ganon, enslavement, destruction, blah blah blah. It's all there, every crucial detail, totally structured for the next agent, bro. This `premise.json` file is just *loaded* with context.
slides.slide_description:
The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top, maintaining the consistent style of the presentation. This slide continues the example of the 'premise.json' file, elaborating on the core story elements after the 'protagonists' section seen in slide 8. The content is presented within a large dark rectangular block, displaying a JSON object.

Key elements within this portion of the JSON include:
-   "protagonists": An array which is shown as closed after the 'Link' entry from the previous slide, indicating this section starts directly after it.
-   "key_antagonist": An object describing "Aghanim" with his "name", "role" ("Evil sorcerer, Ganon's pawn"), and "want" ("To seal the seven maidens, including Princess Zelda, to open a portal to the Dark World and release Ganon").
-   "key_ally": An object describing "Sahasrahla" with his "name", "role" ("Village elder, wise man, guide"), and "want" ("To guide Link to acquire the Master Sword and stop Aghanim's plans to release Ganon").
-   "stakes": A string defining the high stakes of the narrative: "The total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon, resulting in the enslavement or destruction of its inhabitants."

Below the visible JSON, there's cut-off text that reads "CLI to chat CLI to generate", suggesting the method of generation for this premise data. This slide, in conjunction with slides 7 and 8, provides a comprehensive view of how a movie's premise is structured and stored in a JSON format by the Premise Agent.

slides.narration: Okay, so we just got all that amazing context from our `premise.json`, right? Now, bro, next up in our flow is the 'Arc Agent.' This is another chat agent, right? She's gonna discuss your *entire* movie story arc with you. An arc consists of multiple acts, you know? And the cool part is, she takes that `premise.json` we just made as input for *all* her context. So, you tell her what happens in each act, then you're like, 'Yo, draft Act One,' and she'll draft it. One at a time, dude. That `premise.json` file gives her all the overarching story arc context, right? And then, boom, she creates a file called `arc.json`.
slides.slide_description:
The slide is titled "Arc Agent" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top, consistent with the presentation's visual style. A small image of the yellow cardboard robot character, AWESOM-O, is visible in the top right corner. This slide describes the functionality of the 'Arc Agent,' building upon the 'Screenplay Flow' introduced in slide 5 and the 'Premise Agent' from slide 6.

The content is presented with four main black bullet points:

1.  "A chat agent that discusses your movie story arc"
    *   This point is elaborated with two sub-bullets:
        *   "– Arc consists of multiple acts"
        *   "– Agent takes premise.json as input for context"
2.  "You tell the agent what happens in each act, then ask it to draft the act, one at a time"
3.  "Premise file gives it context for the story arc"
4.  "This agent then creates a file arc.json"

The slide explains how the 'Arc Agent' interacts with the user to define the story's high-level plot structure, using the previously generated 'premise.json' for context and ultimately producing an 'arc.json' file, aligning with the hierarchical workflow outlined earlier in the presentation.

slides.narration: Alright, so check out `arc.json`, bro! This is the output from our Arc Agent, right? She's detailing Act 3 here for 'Shadow of the Dark World.' You see the label, 'Battle Aghanim and the Dark World,' and she even estimates it'll be twenty to thirty pages out of a one-hundred-twenty-page script. How cool is that? Then there's the 'dramatic question' for this act, and a whole 'summary' outlining major events, Link getting banished, blah blah blah. And then these 'must_hit_beats,' super important, right? She lays out all the key plot points. This is like, serious structure, dude.
slides.slide_description:
The slide features the title "Arc.json" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top, consistent with the presentation's style. Below the title, a large dark rectangular block displays a JSON object, presenting a detailed example of the 'arc.json' file discussed in the previous slide. This JSON object appears to describe a single act within a larger story arc, specifically 'Act 3' for the 'Shadow of the Dark World' screenplay.

Key elements within the JSON include:
-   "act": 3, indicating the specific act being described.
-   "label": "Battle Aghanim and the Dark World", providing a descriptive title for this act.
-   "approx_pages_note": A note estimating the length of this act to be "20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay."
-   "dramatic_question": Posing the central conflict for this act: "Can Link defeat Aghanim, save Zelda and the other maidens, and prevent Ganon from conquering their world, even when cast into the heart of darkness?"
-   "summary": A lengthy narrative summary detailing the events of Act 3, including Link finding the Master Sword, Zelda being captured, Link confronting Aghanim, Aghanim being wounded, and Link being banished to the Dark World. It also mentions Sahasrahla's telepathic guidance for Link to rescue the maidens.
-   "turning_point_end": "Aghanim banishes Link to the corrupted Dark World, setting the stage for a new, desperate quest as the ultimate evil, Ganon, looms large."
-   "must_hit_beats": An array of bullet points (strings) listing key plot moments and actions that must occur within this act, such as "Link finds the Master Sword" and "Sahasrahla telepathically instructs Link to rescue the seven maidens."
-   "midpoint_note": null.

This slide provides a concrete, detailed example of how a specific act of a screenplay's arc is structured and stored in a JSON format by the Arc Agent, building on the concepts introduced in slide 10.

slides.narration: Okay, so we just got all that awesome `arc.json` from our Arc Agent, right? Now, bro, next up in our flow is the 'Sequence Agent.' And get this: she's an *automated* agent, like, no more chat with this one, dude. She just writes the sequences of the movie for each act, all on her own. What does she need as input? Our `premise.json` and `arc.json` for that big, global view of the movie, *plus* all the previous sequences she's generated so far. That gives her the local context for what should happen next, right? She defines the narrative goal, key locations, and that exit beat, like, how the sequence ends and transitions. And then, boom, she creates a file called `sequence.json`.
slides.slide_description:
The slide is titled "Sequence Agent" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top, consistent with the presentation's style. A small image of the yellow cardboard robot character, AWESOM-O, is visible in the top right corner. This slide describes the functionality of the 'Sequence Agent,' continuing the workflow established by the 'Premise Agent' and 'Arc Agent,' and moving towards generating 'Sequences' as outlined in the 'Screenplay Flow' diagram (slide 5).

The content is presented with four main black bullet points:

1.  "An automated agent (no chat) that writes the sequences of the movie for each act". This highlights a shift from previous chat-based agents.
2.  "Inputs per sequence"
    *   "– Premise.json, arc.json – give agent global view of the movie"
    *   "– All previous sequences generated so far – give agent local context for what should happen next"
3.  "Primary sequence elements"
    *   "– Goal – what is the narrative purpose of this sequence"
    *   "– Key locations – locations in the sequence"
    *   "– Exit beat – how the sequence ends and transitions to next sequence"
4.  "This agent then creates a file sequence.json"

The slide details that this agent operates without direct chat interaction, using the previously generated 'premise.json' and 'arc.json' files for global context, along with any already generated sequences for local context. It focuses on defining the narrative goal, key locations, and transition beats for each sequence, ultimately outputting a 'sequence.json' file.

slides.narration: Alright, so here it is, bro, the `sequence.json` file from our automated Sequence Agent. Look, it's got the project ID, `sequence_count` telling us there are eight sequences for this project. And then for `SEQ1`, which she's titled 'Zelda's Urgent Plea,' she nails the goal, right? Establishing Link's call to adventure, saving his uncle and Zelda, getting his first tools. Even gives us the `emotional_tone`: 'Urgent, sorrowful, determined.' And these `key_locations`, Link's Home, Hyrule Castle, you know? Plus, that `exit_beat`, Link armed and ready to infiltrate. So much detail, right? All perfectly structured for the next agent!
slides.slide_description:
The slide features the title "Sequence.json" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top, consistent with the presentation's style. Below the title, a large dark rectangular block displays a JSON object, presenting an example of the 'sequence.json' file discussed as the output of the 'Sequence Agent' in the previous slide.

The JSON content begins with top-level keys:
-   "project_id": "shadow_of_the_dark_world_20260402_025456"
-   "sequence_count": 8
-   "note": null
-   "sequences": an array containing individual sequence definitions.

The first object within the "sequences" array, representing 'Sequence 1', is fully visible and detailed:
-   "id": "SEQ1"
-   "title": "Zelda's Urgent Plea"
-   "act": 1
-   "goal": "To establish Link's call to adventure, his initial motivation (saving his uncle and Zelda), and to equip him with his first tools for the perilous journey ahead."
-   "emotional_tone": "Urgent, sorrowful, determined"
-   "key_locations": ["Link's Home", "Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls)"]
-   "exit_beat": "Armed with his uncle's sword and shield, Link steels himself, ready to infiltrate the formidable Hyrule Castle and rescue Princess Zelda."

This slide serves as a concrete example of how individual story sequences, complete with narrative goals, emotional tones, key locations, and exit beats, are structured and stored in a JSON format by the automated Sequence Agent, as part of the hierarchical screenplay generation workflow.

slides.narration: And finally, bro, after all that, we're at the 'Scene Agent.' She's another automated agent, no chat needed here, dude. She just writes the individual scenes for the movie, for each sequence. Her inputs are huge: `premise.json`, `arc.json`, `sequence.json` – that gives her the global view, right? Plus, *all* the scenes generated so far, both in the current and previous sequences, for that super critical local context. Her primary scene elements are the slug line, like, the location of the scene, and then 'blocks' of actions and dialogues. And then, boom, she creates the final file: `scenes.json`.
slides.slide_description:
The slide is titled "Scene Agent" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top, consistent with the presentation's style. A small image of the yellow cardboard robot character, AWESOM-O, is visible in the top right corner. This slide describes the functionality of the 'Scene Agent,' continuing the workflow established by the 'Premise Agent,' 'Arc Agent,' and 'Sequence Agent,' and focusing on the final step of generating 'Scenes' as outlined in the 'Screenplay Flow' diagram (slide 5).

The content is presented with four main black bullet points:

1.  "An automated agent (no chat) that writes the scenes of the movie for each sequence". This emphasizes its automated nature, similar to the Sequence Agent and unlike the earlier chat-based agents.
2.  "Inputs per sequence"
    *   "– Premise.json, arc.json, sequence.json – give agent global view of the movie". This indicates the agent uses all previously generated hierarchical data for overall context.
    *   "– All scenes generated so far in this sequence and the previous sequence – give agent local context for what should happen next". This highlights the iterative and contextual nature of scene generation.
3.  "Primary scene elements"
    *   "– Slug line – location of the scene"
    *   "– Blocks – series of actions and dialogues in the scene"
4.  "This agent then creates a file scenes.json". This specifies the final output file, completing the hierarchical screenplay generation process envisioned by the 'Screenplay Flow' chart (slide 5).

slides.narration: Alright, so, dude, this is it! `scenes.json`, the grand finale from our Scene Agent. This is like, an actual opening scene from 'Shadow of the Dark World,' right? You see the `slugline`: `INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT`. And then these `blocks`, bro, they break down the scene. Link sleeping peacefully, then a piercing whisper, he jolts awake. Boom, Zelda's voice-over, begging for help, trapped by Aghanim. Then Link's getting visions of Hyrule Castle, Zelda's terrified face, the sorcerer. All perfectly structured action and dialogue, totally coherent, right? All thanks to that rich context from the previous agents.
slides.slide_description:
The slide features the title "Scenes.json" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top, consistent with the presentation's style. Below the title, a large dark rectangular block displays a JSON object, presenting a detailed example of the 'scenes.json' file, which is the output of the 'Scene Agent' discussed in the previous slide (slide 14).

The JSON content outlines the structure of a single scene, likely the opening scene of the 'Shadow of the Dark World' screenplay:
-   "id": "S0001"
-   "slug_id": "SEQ1-01", indicating it's the first scene of Sequence 1.
-   "slugline": "INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT", a standard screenplay heading specifying the indoor location and time.
-   "line_start": null
-   "sequence_id": "SEQ1"
-   "blocks": An array containing multiple content blocks, detailing the actions and dialogue within the scene:
    -   An "action" block describing Link sleeping in his 'simple, familiar room', lit by a lantern's soft glow.
    -   A second "action" block where a 'piercing, desperate WHISPER' echoes in Link's mind.
    -   A third "action" block where Link 'jolts awake, eyes wide, disoriented' as the whisper intensifies.
    -   A "dialogue" block from "ZELDA (V.O.)" (Voice Over), where she pleads: "Link... help me... I'm trapped in the castle... Aghanim... has me..."
    -   A final "action" block where Link clutches his head, experiencing visions of 'HYRULE CASTLE, Princess ZELDA's terrified face, the menacing silhouette of a SORCERER'.

This slide provides a concrete, block-by-block example of how a scene, including both action descriptions and dialogue, is structured and stored in a JSON format by the automated Scene Agent, directly connecting to the narrative elements established in earlier 'premise.json', 'arc.json', and 'sequence.json' files.

slides.narration: Alright, so we've got our whole screenplay flow, right? We've got `scenes.json` all generated and everything. But what if, like, the first draft isn't totally perfect, bro? That's where these 'Enhancement Agents' come in! We can totally improve this entire agentic flow by sending in specialized agents to enhance those intermediate outputs. So, she can write better sequences or scenes, clean up that initial premise if it's a bit blah, or even make the arc way more creative, you know? See those little robot dudes on the arrows in the diagram? They're stepping in *between* stages to refine things, make it awesome. It's iterative refinement, dude!
slides.slide_description:
The slide is titled "Enhancement Agents" in large, bold dark blue text at the top, consistent with the presentation's style. The main content explains the role of these agents in improving the generation process. A black bullet point states: "We can improve the agentic flow by using agents to enhance intermediate outputs". This is followed by three sub-bullets that provide examples: "– Write better sequences or scenes", "– Clean up the premise", and "– Make the arc more creative".

To the right of the bullet points, an illustration depicts a golden robot with blue eyes, reminiscent of the AWESOM-O character, and labeled "ENHANCEMENT AGENT" on its chest.

At the bottom of the slide, a flow diagram visually demonstrates how these enhancement agents integrate into the previously established screenplay generation workflow, echoing the 'Screenplay Flow' diagram from slide 5. It features four distinct, colorful rectangular boxes, each with a chest icon and a corresponding '.json' filename:
*   A blue box labeled "Premise" with "premise.json" below it.
*   A purple box labeled "Arc" with "arc.json" below it.
*   An orange box labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json" below it.
*   A green box labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json" below it.

Crucially, small golden robot icons, representing the enhancement agents, are placed along the arrows connecting these stages: an agent is shown between "Premise" and "Arc", another between "Arc" and "Sequences", and a third between "Arc" and "Scenes". This visual representation clearly illustrates the concept of enhancement agents intervening to refine the output of one stage before it progresses to the next, reinforcing the textual description of improving intermediate outputs.

slides.narration: Alright, so we've got this whole incredible screenplay generation thing going, right? And we even got our Enhancement Agents making it even better. But here's the kicker, bro, the true genius of this whole setup: you can totally generalize this to *any* other long text document! Check out the table. By just modifying those agent prompts, like, for a book, your Arc becomes the table of contents, your Sequence is a chapter, and a Scene is a sub-chapter. For a podcast, dude, it's Concept, Flow, Segment, Beat. Even a lecture, right? Learning Objective, Outline, Module, Slide. It's all just about mapping those hierarchical elements. How cool is that?
slides.slide_description:
The slide is titled "Generalizing to Other Documents" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top. This slide aims to show the broader applicability of the hierarchical agentic flow discussed in previous slides for screenplays.

Below the title, a black bullet point states: "By modifying the agent prompts, this hierarchical flow can be used for other long text documents".

The main content of the slide is a large table with six columns and five data rows, plus a header row. The header row has a dark blue background and white text, labeling the columns: "Document", "Premise", "Arc", "Sequence", "Scene", and "Artist Rendering".

Each row represents a different type of document, mapping its structural elements to the hierarchical levels (Premise, Arc, Sequence, Scene) previously used for screenplays, and includes a cartoon illustration in the 'Artist Rendering' column, all featuring the yellow AWESOM-O robot character:

1.  **Book:** Premise: "Premise", Arc: "Table of contents", Sequence: "Chapter", Scene: "Sub-chapter". The rendering shows AWESOM-O reading a book.
2.  **Podcast:** Premise: "Concept", Arc: "Flow, format", Sequence: "Segment", Scene: "Beat". The rendering shows AWESOM-O producing a podcast.
3.  **Song:** Premise: "Premise", Arc: "Structure (verse, hooks, coda)", Sequence: "Verse", Scene: "Couplet". The rendering shows AWESOM-O performing on stage.
4.  **Lecture:** Premise: "Learning objective", Arc: "Outline", Sequence: "Module", Scene: "Slide". The rendering shows AWESOM-O giving a lecture in a classroom.
5.  **Business plan:** Premise: "Value prop", Arc: "Outline", Sequence: "Section", Scene: "Sub-section". The rendering shows AWESOM-O presenting a business plan in a boardroom.

This table visually demonstrates how the same agentic framework can be adapted by changing the 'prompts' to generate structured content for various document types beyond screenplays.

slides.narration: Alright, so, we just saw how powerful this whole setup is, right? We can generalize it to anything! Now, dude, for the coding session, this is where we actually get our hands dirty. We're gonna write a short screenplay, a whole one, using Awesome-O, live. Then, we'll actually integrate those cool enhancement agents into our flow to make it even *more* polished. And finally, bro, we're gonna modify Awesome-O, totally adapting her for different document types, just like we saw. You can see the full flow diagram there, right? That's our blueprint, dude. Let's build!
slides.slide_description:
The slide is titled "Coding Session" in large, bold dark blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, three black bullet points outline activities or objectives related to the presentation's themes:
*   "Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O"
*   "Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow"
*   "Modify Awesome-O for different document types"

Below the bullet points, a colorful flow diagram visually summarizes the agentic workflow for generating screenplays, similar to diagrams on previous slides (5 and 16). It consists of four rectangular boxes, each representing a stage in the document generation process, with an associated '.json' file and small AWESOM-O robot and chest icons in their top-left corners:
*   A blue box labeled "Premise" with "premise.json" below it.
*   A purple box labeled "Arc" with "arc.json" below it.
*   An orange box labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json" below it.
*   A green box labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json" below it.

Arrows connect these stages: an arrow goes from "Premise" to "Arc", another from "Arc" to "Sequences", and a third from "Arc" downwards to "Scenes". Each of these connecting arrows also features a small AWESOM-O robot icon, reinforcing the concept of agents facilitating the transitions and processes. This diagram serves as a visual summary of the hierarchical, agent-driven content generation process discussed throughout the lecture.
